title: Deepfake & AI Impersonation Law Tracker
description: Federal statutes, rules, enforcement orders, and state laws addressing deepfakes, AI-generated impersonation, synthetic NCII, and digital replicas, with citations, effective dates, primary sources, and litigation status. Not legal advice; confirm against the linked primary sources.
citation: Vorp Labs, Deepfake & AI Impersonation Law Tracker (as of 2026-07-22), https://vorplabs.com/ai-regulatory-updates/deepfake-laws

keyDates.label: Platform compliance deadline (365 days from enactment)

federalInstruments.obligations: Covered platforms must remove intimate images, including AI-generated synthetic intimate images, within 48 hours of a valid report. Platforms had until 2026-05-19 to stand up removal processes.
federalInstruments.penalties: FTC enforces under FTC Act Section 5. Civil penalties per 16 CFR 1.98 ($53,088 per violation as of the 2024 adjustment). Criminal penalties for knowing distribution of non-consensual intimate images, including deepfake images, with up to two years imprisonment.
federalInstruments.statusDetail: Signed 2025-04-28. Platform compliance deadline passed 2026-05-19. FTC stood up TakeItDown.ftc.gov as a reporting portal. No public FTC enforcement cases under the Act as of 2026-07-10.

factChecks.label: Statute text
factChecks.value: TAKE IT DOWN Act enacted as Public Law 119-12, requiring covered platforms to remove reported intimate images within 48 hours.

factChecks.label: FTC reporting portal
factChecks.value: FTC launched TakeItDown.ftc.gov as the reporting mechanism for the Act.

factChecks.label: Civil penalty amount
factChecks.value: Per 16 CFR 1.98, the FTC civil penalty for violations of rules under the FTC Act is $53,088 per violation (2024 adjustment).

openItems: Exact computation of 365-day platform deadline (2026-05-19) not independently verified against the statute text; based on reported enactment date of 2025-04-28.
No FTC enforcement cases under the TAKE IT DOWN Act were found on the FTC legal library as of 2026-07-10.

federalInstruments.instrument: Government and Business Impersonation Rule (16 CFR Part 461)
federalInstruments.obligations: Makes impersonation of government entities and businesses, including via AI-generated content, an unfair or deceptive act under the FTC Act. Enables the FTC to seek civil penalties and consumer redress.
federalInstruments.penalties: Civil penalties under FTC Act Section 5(m)(1)(A); consumer redress under Section 19. The individual-impersonation extension was proposed as an SNPRM but has not been finalized.
federalInstruments.statusDetail: Government and business impersonation provisions effective 2024-04-01. The proposed extension to individual impersonation (SNPRM) is not finalized as of 2026-07-10.

factChecks.label: Rule text
factChecks.value: 16 CFR Part 461, Trade Regulation Rule on Impersonation of Government and Businesses is codified at eCFR, effective 2024-04-01.

openItems: Individual-impersonation SNPRM status: proposed but not finalized as of 2026-07-10. Check Federal Register for final rule publication.

federalInstruments.instrument: FCC Declaratory Ruling: AI-Generated Voices Under TCPA
federalInstruments.obligations: Clarifies that AI-generated voices are "artificial" voices under 47 U.S.C. 227(b)(1), making AI-voice robocalls to residential lines without prior express consent violations of the TCPA.
federalInstruments.penalties: Existing TCPA penalties apply: up to $500 per violation, trebled to $1,500 for willful or knowing violations. State attorneys general may also enforce.
federalInstruments.statusDetail: Declaratory ruling released 2024-02-08. Immediately effective; it clarifies existing TCPA authority rather than creating new rules.

factChecks.label: Ruling text
factChecks.value: FCC 24-17 declares that calls made with AI-generated human voices are "artificial" under 47 U.S.C. 227(b)(1)(A) and subject to TCPA prior-express-consent requirements.

federalInstruments.instrument: FCC NPRM: AI-Generated Calls and Texts
federalInstruments.obligations: Proposes additional rules for AI-generated calls and texts, including disclosure requirements and consent frameworks. Not finalized.
federalInstruments.penalties: Not applicable (proposed rule, not in force).
federalInstruments.statusDetail: NPRM released 2024-08-08. Comment period closed. No final rule issued as of 2026-07-10.

factChecks.label: NPRM text
factChecks.value: FCC 24-84 proposes rules for AI-generated calls and texts. The NPRM is not finalized.

openItems: Final rule status: NPRM comment period closed; no final rule published as of 2026-07-10.

federalInstruments.instrument: FCC Consent Decree: Lingo Telecom (AI Deepfake Robocalls)
federalInstruments.obligations: Requires Lingo Telecom to pay $1,000,000 civil penalty, implement compliance plan, and adopt Know Your Customer / Know Your Upstream Provider controls for STIR/SHAKEN attestation.
federalInstruments.statusDetail: Consent decree entered 2024-08-21 for STIR/SHAKEN attestation violations tied to transmission of AI deepfake robocalls impersonating President Biden.

factChecks.label: Consent decree
factChecks.value: FCC Enforcement Bureau entered a consent decree with Lingo Telecom, File No. EB-TCD-24-00036425, requiring $1,000,000 civil penalty for STIR/SHAKEN attestation violations tied to deepfake AI robocalls.

federalInstruments.instrument: FCC Forfeiture Order: Steve Kramer (AI Deepfake Robocalls)
federalInstruments.obligations: $6,000,000 forfeiture for illegal robocalls carrying deepfake AI-generated voice messages impersonating President Biden, in violation of the Truth in Caller ID Act.
federalInstruments.statusDetail: Forfeiture order adopted 2024-09-26, released 2024-09-30. FCC referred Kramer to DOJ for collection 2024-12-03.

factChecks.label: Forfeiture order
factChecks.value: FCC imposed a $6,000,000 penalty against Steve Kramer for illegal robocalls carrying a deepfake generative AI voice message imitating President Biden, in violation of the Truth in Caller ID Act.

factChecks.label: DOJ referral
factChecks.value: FCC reported Kramer referral to DOJ for collection on 2024-12-03.

federalInstruments.obligations: Would create a federal civil cause of action for victims of non-consensual deepfake intimate images, with statutory damages and injunctive relief. Not enacted.
federalInstruments.penalties: Not applicable (bill, not law). Would provide statutory damages if enacted.
federalInstruments.statusDetail: Passed the Senate 2026-01-13. Not passed by the House. Not enacted as of 2026-07-10.

factChecks.label: Senate passage
factChecks.value: S.1837 (DEFIANCE Act) passed the Senate on 2026-01-13. The bill has not been enacted into law.

openItems: House action status: no House companion or floor vote identified as of 2026-07-10.

federalInstruments.obligations: Would create a federal right of publicity for AI-generated digital replicas of voice and likeness. Not enacted.
federalInstruments.penalties: Not applicable (bill, not law). Would provide statutory damages and injunctive relief if enacted.
federalInstruments.statusDetail: Introduced in the 118th Congress; reintroduced in the 119th. Not enacted as of 2026-07-10.

factChecks.label: Bill status
factChecks.value: The NO FAKES Act has been introduced but not enacted. It remains pending in Congress.

stateLaws.scope: Prohibits distribution of deceptive deepfake audio or video of candidates within 60 days of an election with intent to injure the candidate's reputation or deceive a voter.
stateLaws.penalty: No criminal penalty. Injunctive relief; civil liability.
stateLaws.privateRightOfAction: Yes, candidates may seek injunctive relief.

factChecks.label: Bill text
factChecks.value: AB 730 added Elections Code Section 20010, prohibiting distribution of materially deceptive audio or visual media of candidates within 60 days of an election.

stateLaws.scope: Signed into law 2024-09-17. Extends the deepfake election prohibition to 120 days before an election, adds platform takedown duties for deceptive AI-generated election content, and expands injunctive and damages remedies. A federal court has temporarily blocked some of these provisions on First Amendment grounds; which parts are currently enforceable depends on the latest court orders.
stateLaws.penalty: Injunctive relief; civil damages. Platforms face mandatory content labeling and removal obligations (subject to court orders).
stateLaws.privateRightOfAction: Yes.
stateLaws.litigationStatus: A federal court (E.D. Cal.) issued a preliminary injunction blocking portions of AB 2839 in Kohls v. Bonta (2:24-cv-02527). X Corp v. Bonta raised similar First Amendment challenges. Which provisions remain enforceable depends on the most recent court orders; check the docket for current status.

factChecks.label: Enacted text
factChecks.value: AB 2839 was signed into law and took effect 2024-09-17. It expands the deepfake election prohibition window and adds platform duties.

factChecks.label: Litigation caveat
factChecks.value: Portions of AB 2839 are subject to preliminary injunction in Kohls v. Bonta (E.D. Cal. 2:24-cv-02527). The current scope of enforceable provisions is docket-dependent and may change.

openItems: Current scope of Kohls v. Bonta preliminary injunction requires PACER docket review. Operative provisions may differ from the enacted text.
X Corp v. Bonta litigation status not independently confirmed as of 2026-07-10.

stateLaws.scope: Enacted text would require large online platforms to label or remove deceptive AI-generated election content. Companion bill to AB 2839. Like AB 2839, portions may be affected by ongoing federal court litigation; current enforceability depends on the latest court orders.
stateLaws.penalty: Civil penalties for platform non-compliance (subject to court orders).
stateLaws.privateRightOfAction: Platform-directed duties; individual claims are under AB 2839.
stateLaws.litigationStatus: May be affected by the same federal court litigation challenging AB 2839. Which platform duties are currently enforceable depends on the most recent court orders; check the AB 2839 docket for status.

factChecks.label: Enacted text
factChecks.value: AB 2655 requires large online platforms to label or remove deceptive AI-generated election content. Signed into law alongside AB 2839.

openItems: Enforceability may be affected by AB 2839 litigation. Current status not independently confirmed.

stateLaws.scope: First state deepfake election law. Prohibits creating or distributing a deepfake video intended to injure a candidate or influence an election within 30 days of an election.
stateLaws.penalty: Class A misdemeanor.
stateLaws.privateRightOfAction: Not an express private right; criminal enforcement.

factChecks.label: Statute text
factChecks.value: Texas Election Code 255.004 (added by SB 751, 86th Legislature) prohibits creation or distribution of deepfake videos intended to injure a candidate or influence an election within 30 days of the election.

stateLaws.scope: Criminal prohibition on using deepfake technology to influence elections. Prohibits disseminating deepfake media depicting a candidate within 90 days of an election without disclosure.
stateLaws.penalty: Tiered criminal penalties: up to 90 days or $1,000 in the base case; up to 364 days or $3,000 where there is intent to cause violence or bodily harm; up to five years or $10,000 for a repeat offense within five years. A convicted candidate forfeits the nomination or office.
stateLaws.privateRightOfAction: The depicted individual, an injured candidate, the attorney general, or a county or city attorney may seek injunctive relief; the offense itself is criminal.
stateLaws.litigationStatus: Challenged on First Amendment grounds. Current litigation status not independently confirmed as of 2026-07-10.

factChecks.label: Statute text
factChecks.value: Minnesota Statute 609.771 criminalizes disseminating a deep fake without consent, with intent to injure a candidate or influence an election, within 90 days of a nominating convention or after absentee voting begins, with tiered penalties and candidate forfeiture.

openItems: First Amendment challenge status not independently confirmed. Check court records for current litigation posture.

stateLaws.scope: Prohibits distribution of synthetic media depicting a candidate in a false manner in an election campaign without a disclosure label.
stateLaws.penalty: Injunctive relief; civil penalties. Candidates may seek damages.
stateLaws.privateRightOfAction: Yes, candidates may seek injunctive relief and damages.

factChecks.label: Bill text
factChecks.value: SB 5152 adds provisions to Washington election law requiring disclosure of synthetic media used in campaigns.

stateLaws.scope: Requires AI-generated content in political advertisements and electioneering communications to include a specified disclaimer.
stateLaws.penalty: Criminal and civil penalties for missing disclaimers, with a complaint process and expedited hearings.
stateLaws.privateRightOfAction: A person may file a complaint under the act's expedited procedures; candidates depicted may seek relief.

factChecks.label: Bill page
factChecks.value: HB 919 (Artificial Intelligence Use in Political Advertising) was approved April 26, 2024 as chapter 2024-126, effective July 1, 2024, requiring disclaimers on AI-generated political ads with criminal and civil penalties.

stateLaws.scope: Creates a cause of action against the sponsor of an electioneering communication that uses synthetic media to alter a candidate's appearance, action, or speech without a clear and conspicuous disclosure. The act also establishes AI governance requirements for Kentucky state government; the election provisions amend KRS 117.001 and add KRS 117.322.
stateLaws.penalty: No standalone criminal offense for the synthetic media itself. Failure to comply with a court-ordered disclosure is subject to the campaign finance penalties in KRS 121.990(3). Media outlets and their advertising representatives are generally not liable, with limited exceptions.
stateLaws.privateRightOfAction: Yes; an affected candidate may seek injunctive or other equitable relief requiring the disclosure, with attorney fees and costs available to the prevailing party, on clear and convincing evidence.

factChecks.label: Enacted bill
factChecks.value: SB 4 (2025 session) was signed March 24, 2025 as 2025 Ky. Acts ch. 66 with an emergency clause making it effective on approval. Its election provisions define synthetic media in electioneering communications and create the KRS 117.322 disclosure action.

factChecks.label: Codified section
factChecks.value: KRS 117.322 codifies the action against use of synthetic media in electioneering communications, including remedies, defenses, and exceptions.

openItems: The codified KRS 117.322 page carries a July 15, 2026 effective notation that suggests a later amendment to the section; the 2025 act's emergency clause governs the original enactment. Confirm the 2026 amendment at the next check.

stateLaws.scope: Requires a clear and understandable disclosure on electioneering communications that use artificial intelligence to create, alter, or digitally manipulate the image, likeness, or voice of a candidate or of an elected official subject to recall so that it falsely appears to a reasonable observer to be an authentic record of actual speech or conduct. Signed June 9, 2026; the tracker previously miscited the unrelated 2025 HB 459.
stateLaws.penalty: Criminal: violating R.S. 18:1463 with intent to injure the reputation of a candidate or elected official subject to recall, or to otherwise deceive a voter, carries a fine of up to $2,000, imprisonment for up to two years with or without hard labor, or both. Media entities that merely broadcast paid content over which they have no editorial control are exempt.
stateLaws.privateRightOfAction: No express private right of action in the AI disclosure provisions; enforcement is criminal.

factChecks.label: Bill page
factChecks.value: HB 459 (2026 Regular Session) became Act 806, signed June 9, 2026 and effective August 1, 2026, amending R.S. 18:1463 and 18:1483 to require AI-manipulation disclosures on electioneering communications.

factChecks.label: Enrolled act text
factChecks.value: The enrolled Act 806 text amends R.S. 18:1463(A), (F), and (G) and related definitions in R.S. 18:1483, and enacts R.S. 18:1463(H) and (I) covering AI-manipulated candidate media.

openItems: The August 1, 2026 effective date is stated on the official bill page; the enrolled text has no separate effective-date section, consistent with Louisiana's default for regular-session acts.

stateLaws.scope: Requires campaign advertising that contains synthetic media of a candidate, manipulated or AI-generated content likely to deceive a reasonable person about the candidate's appearance, speech, or conduct, to carry the disclosure 'THIS COMMUNICATION CONTAINS AUDIO, VIDEO AND/OR IMAGES THAT HAVE BEEN MANIPULATED OR ALTERED', with satire and parody excluded. Carried over from the 2025 session and signed March 23, 2026.
stateLaws.penalty: Civil penalties assessed by the Commission on Governmental Ethics and Election Practices: up to 500% of the expenditure amount for a synthetic-media disclosure violation, versus the 100% cap for other Section 1014 violations. The Attorney General may seek injunctive relief and recover costs and fees.
stateLaws.privateRightOfAction: No new private right of action; the law does not limit other causes of action a person may have.

factChecks.label: Bill history
factChecks.value: LD 517 (HP 335, 132nd Legislature) was carried over, amended by Committee Amendment A (H-837), enacted, and signed March 23, 2026 as Public Law chapter 593, amending 21-A M.R.S. 1014 and enacting subsection 5-C.

factChecks.label: Effective date
factChecks.value: As a nonemergency law it takes effect 90 days after adjournment of the Second Regular Session (sine die April 29, 2026), which the Legislature states as July 29, 2026.

stateLaws.scope: Prohibits knowingly, or with reckless disregard, creating, using, or disseminating a deepfake to produce materially false information with intent to influence voting, misrepresent election facts, or induce petition signatures, with satire, parody, broadcaster, and Section 230 exemptions. Also directs the State Administrator of Elections to correct credible reports of election misinformation and authorizes injunctions and subpoenas in limited cases. Approved May 12, 2026; the tracker previously miscited the withdrawn 2025 SB 141.
stateLaws.penalty: Misdemeanor: a fine of up to $5,000, imprisonment for up to five years, or both. The State Board of Elections may also bring a civil action to recover correction costs, damages, court costs, and attorney fees on clear and convincing evidence.
stateLaws.privateRightOfAction: No general private right of action; enforcement runs through the State Administrator, the State Board, and criminal prosecution.

factChecks.label: Bill page
factChecks.value: SB 141 (2026 Regular Session) was approved May 12, 2026 as Chapter 444, amending Election Law 2-110 and adding Election Law 16-905 on election misinformation, disinformation, and deepfakes.

factChecks.label: Enrolled text
factChecks.value: Section 2 of the enrolled bill states the act takes effect June 1, 2026. The deepfake offense in Election Law 16-905 is a misdemeanor punishable by up to $5,000, up to five years, or both.

stateLaws.scope: General digital-forgery offense, not an election-specific statute: criminalizes creating and distributing as genuine a forged digital likeness, a computer-generated visual or audio representation of an identifiable individual that materially misrepresents their appearance, speech, or behavior and is likely to deceive a reasonable person, made without consent and with intent to defraud or injure. Deceptive political deepfakes can fall within it only when those general elements are met.
stateLaws.penalty: First-degree misdemeanor generally; third-degree felony when committed through a scheme to defraud, coerce, or commit theft. Affirmative defense where the defendant took reasonable action to put viewers or listeners on notice that the content is not genuine; exceptions for constitutionally protected activity, law enforcement duties, and technology providers.
stateLaws.privateRightOfAction: No; criminal enforcement only.

factChecks.label: Bill page
factChecks.value: SB 649 (2025-2026 Regular Session) was approved July 7, 2025 as Act 35 of 2025, adding the digital-forgery offense at 18 Pa.C.S. 4101.1. Its Section 2 sets a 60-day effective date, September 5, 2025.

factChecks.label: Act information
factChecks.value: The official act record confirms Act 35 of 2025 with a 60-day effective clause and no election-specific elements; the offense sits in Title 18's forgery and fraudulent practices chapter.

stateLaws.scope: Criminalizes distributing materially deceptive media with intent to influence an upcoming election, subject to statutory exceptions, and permits permanent injunctive relief.
stateLaws.privateRightOfAction: Yes, limited to permanent injunctive relief for statutorily authorized plaintiffs.

factChecks.label: Enrolled bill
factChecks.value: The enrolled HB 172 criminalizes materially deceptive election media and authorizes permanent injunctive relief.

openItems: Final Alabama Code section, act number, offense classification, and effective date pending code-level verification.

stateLaws.scope: During the 90 days before an election, a creator may not knowingly create and distribute a deceptive and fraudulent deepfake of a candidate on the ballot unless the media conspicuously discloses that it contains AI-generated content.
stateLaws.penalty: Civil penalty under A.R.S. 16-937(B) for each day the undisclosed deepfake is distributed.
stateLaws.privateRightOfAction: No express private cause of action.

factChecks.label: Session law
factChecks.value: SB 1359 was enacted as 2024 Ariz. Sess. Laws ch. 199, adding A.R.S. 16-1023 with a 90-day pre-election disclosure requirement for candidate deepfakes, effective September 14, 2024.

stateLaws.scope: Prohibits knowingly or recklessly distributing an undisclosed or improperly disclosed deepfake communication about a candidate during the 60 days before a primary or 90 days before a general election.
stateLaws.penalty: Administrative civil penalty of at least $100 per violation for unpaid advertising, or at least 10% of the amount paid for paid advertising.
stateLaws.privateRightOfAction: Yes; a depicted candidate may seek an injunction, damages including punitive damages, fees, and costs.

factChecks.label: Bill page
factChecks.value: HB 24-1147 was enacted and codified at C.R.S. 1-46-101 through 1-46-106, effective July 1, 2024.

stateLaws.scope: From the first working day of February in even-numbered years through the general election, prohibits reckless distribution of materially deceptive media that risks harming a candidate or changing voting behavior, subject to exceptions.
stateLaws.privateRightOfAction: Yes; a depicted individual or qualifying voter organization may obtain damages and equitable relief, and violating a court order can draw a civil fine of up to $1,000 per day.

factChecks.label: Final bill text
factChecks.value: SB 2687 CD1 adds two sections to HRS chapter 11, part XIII restricting materially deceptive election media, effective upon approval.

openItems: Enacted revisor section numbers, act number, exact approval date, and offense grading pending confirmation from the complete enacted text.

stateLaws.scope: Election deepfake regulation.
openItems: The official legislature endpoint could not be retrieved at the July 22, 2026 check; the bill number and all substantive fields are unverified pending recheck.

stateLaws.scope: Requires a prescribed disclaimer on campaign communications containing fabricated media that depicts a candidate, including materially altered recordings and lifelike AI imitations.
stateLaws.penalty: No standalone criminal fine; a depicted candidate may seek actual damages, injunctive relief, attorney fees, and costs against responsible sponsors or payors.
stateLaws.privateRightOfAction: Yes.

factChecks.label: Bill page
factChecks.value: HB 1133 was enacted as P.L. 81-2024, adding Ind. Code 3-9-8 fabricated-media disclaimer requirements effective March 12, 2024 upon passage.

openItems: Prior tracker citation SEA 114 was replaced with HB 1133 after the July 22, 2026 primary-source review; final code-site recheck of Ind. Code 3-9-8 pending.

stateLaws.scope: Prohibits knowingly disseminating a nonconsensual digitization within 90 days of an election when intended to injure a candidate, influence an election result, or deter voting.
stateLaws.privateRightOfAction: Yes, for statutorily authorized injunctive relief.

factChecks.label: Session law text
factChecks.value: SB 2577 restricts nonconsensual digitizations in the 90 days before an election, effective July 1, 2024, with criminal penalties and enhanced treatment for repeat conduct.

openItems: Final Mississippi Code section, chapter number, and precise penalty grading pending confirmation from the complete signed text.

stateLaws.scope: Requires disclaimers on AI-generated campaign advertisements and prohibits knowing distribution of materially deceptive election media under the Campaign Reporting Act.
stateLaws.privateRightOfAction: Yes, at least for statutory injunctive relief.

factChecks.label: Bill page
factChecks.value: HB 182 was enacted as 2024 N.M. Laws ch. 57, effective March 5, 2024 upon signing under an emergency clause.

openItems: Final NMSA revisor section numbers and precise penalty amounts pending confirmation from the final chapter text.

stateLaws.scope: Requires disclosure when a political communication contains materially deceptive media and the distributor or publisher has actual knowledge of the deception, with satire, news, broadcaster, platform, and federal-law exceptions.
stateLaws.penalty: No standalone monetary fine in subdivision 5; enforcement is principally expedited injunctive relief with recoverable court costs and attorney fees.
stateLaws.privateRightOfAction: Yes; a candidate whose voice or likeness appears in the media may bring the expedited action.

factChecks.label: Codified statute
factChecks.value: Election Law 14-106(5), enacted through the 2024 budget bill (L. 2024 ch. 58, Part MM), requires disclosure of materially deceptive media in political communications; the codified page reflects a February 6, 2026 revision.

openItems: Prior tracker citation S7694 was replaced with the enacted budget-bill citation after the July 22, 2026 primary-source review.

stateLaws.scope: Requires campaign communications containing synthetic media to disclose that the image, audio, or video has been manipulated, with platform, news, broadcaster, periodical, satire, and parody exceptions.
stateLaws.penalty: Court-imposed civil penalty of up to $10,000 per violation; the statutory enforcement remedy is exclusive.
stateLaws.privateRightOfAction: No general private right; enforcement is assigned to the Secretary of State or Attorney General.

factChecks.label: Bill overview
factChecks.value: SB 1571 was enacted as 2024 Or. Laws ch. 62 effective March 27, 2024 under an emergency clause, adding a synthetic-media disclosure requirement to ORS chapter 260 and amending ORS 260.345.

openItems: Final ORS section number assigned to the new provision pending recheck; the session-law citation controls until then.

stateLaws.scope: Requires audible, visual, and digital-provenance disclosures for paid audio or visual communications intended to influence voting on a candidate or ballot proposition when the communication contains synthetic media. The disclosure duties took effect May 1, 2024; the sentencing aggravator took effect July 1, 2024.
stateLaws.penalty: Civil penalty of up to $1,000 per violation; knowing use of AI to facilitate a crime is a sentencing aggravator under 76-3-203.18, effective July 1, 2024.
stateLaws.privateRightOfAction: Yes; the statute permits a civil action to enforce the disclosure section.

factChecks.label: Enrolled bill
factChecks.value: SB 131 added Utah Code 20A-11-1104 (effective May 1, 2024) and 76-3-203.18 (effective July 1, 2024) covering synthetic-media election disclosures and AI-facilitated-crime sentencing.

stateLaws.scope: Requires a prescribed disclosure when a political communication sponsored by a covered person or committee contains synthetic media.
openItems: The official statute page was not retrievable at the July 22, 2026 check; the reported forfeiture of up to $1,000 per violation and the March 23, 2024 effective date await confirmation on the live page.

stateLaws.scope: Requires disclosures for qualifying political advertisements created in whole or substantial part using generative AI, as part of Michigan's 2023 multi-bill election-deepfake package (HBs 5141 to 5145).

factChecks.label: Bill page
factChecks.value: HB 5141 amends the Michigan Campaign Finance Act to require AI-generated content disclosures on qualifying political advertisements; the broader prohibitions sit in the companion bills of the package.

openItems: The reported February 13, 2024 operative date, final act mapping, and exact penalty subdivisions pending confirmation on the chaptered text; do not cite HB 5141 alone for the full Michigan regime.

stateLaws.scope: Treats AI-generated digital replicas and deceptive government-style social-media content as election deception or forgery when reasonably likely to prevent or dissuade registering, voting, or supporting a candidate or public question; not a general political-ad disclosure law.
stateLaws.penalty: Conduct satisfying 10 ILCS 5/29-4 is a Class 4 felony.
stateLaws.privateRightOfAction: Only through the existing 10 ILCS 5/29-18 conspiracy liability framework, not a standalone deepfake cause of action.

factChecks.label: Bill text
factChecks.value: HB 4624 amends 10 ILCS 5/29-4(b) and 5/29-18(a) to cover AI-generated digital replicas within election deception and forgery offenses.

openItems: Public Act number and signing date pending confirmation; prior tracker citation SB 2123 was replaced after the July 22, 2026 primary-source review.

stateLaws.scope: General criminal and civil liability for creating or disseminating deceptive audio or visual media for specified unlawful purposes. The statute is broader than elections and is not a dedicated pre-election deepfake rule, but it reaches deceptive election media used for covered unlawful purposes.
stateLaws.privateRightOfAction: Yes, under the civil-remedy section, 2C:21-17.8.

factChecks.label: Chapter law
factChecks.value: A3540 was enacted as P.L. 2025, c. 40, establishing criminal liability under 2C:21-17.7 and civil remedies under 2C:21-17.8 for deceptive audio or visual media.

openItems: Final effective-date clause pending confirmation (April 2, 2025 if immediately effective). The election-specific S2543 was not verified as enacted; prior tracker citation A4892 was replaced after the July 22, 2026 primary-source review.

stateLaws.scope: During the 90 days before an election, bars distributing an AI-generated deepfake of a candidate, election official, or party on a state or local ballot when the distributor knew or should have known it was a deepfake, unless the required disclosure is included.
stateLaws.penalty: Primarily equitable and civil relief rather than a standalone administrative fine.
stateLaws.privateRightOfAction: Yes; a depicted candidate or election official may seek injunctive or equitable relief and damages against the sponsor, with fees and costs available.

factChecks.label: Bill text
factChecks.value: HB 1596 was enacted as 2024 N.H. Laws ch. 345, adding RSA 664:14-c effective August 1, 2024.

stateLaws.scope: Criminalizes creation and distribution of AI-generated non-consensual intimate images (synthetic sexual content).
stateLaws.penalty: State jail felony (up to two years, $10,000 fine).

factChecks.label: Statute text
factChecks.value: Texas Penal Code Chapter 21, Section 21.165 addresses unlawful creation and distribution of synthetic sexual imagery.

stateLaws.scope: Civil remedies for victims of non-consensual intimate images, including AI-generated intimate images.
stateLaws.penalty: Civil liability; damages and injunctive relief.
stateLaws.privateRightOfAction: Yes.

factChecks.label: Statute text
factChecks.value: Delaware Title 10, Chapter 78 provides civil remedies for non-consensual pornography.

stateLaws.scope: Criminalizes knowingly or purposely disclosing explicit synthetic media, deepfake intimate imagery of an identifiable individual created or altered with AI, without consent, including threats to disclose for money or other consideration.
stateLaws.penalty: First offense: fine up to $1,000, up to one year in county jail, or both. Second or subsequent offense: fine up to $10,000, up to five years in state prison, or both; up to ten years where the depicted person is under 18.

factChecks.label: Statute text
factChecks.value: Montana Code Annotated 45-5-640 (Disclosing Explicit Synthetic Media) criminalizes nonconsensual disclosure of deepfake intimate imagery with tiered penalties.

stateLaws.scope: Civil liability for distribution of sexually explicit deepfake images or recordings created without consent.
stateLaws.penalty: Civil damages, including economic and non-economic damages, punitive damages, and attorney fees.
stateLaws.privateRightOfAction: Yes.

factChecks.label: Statute text
factChecks.value: California Civil Code Section 1708.86 creates civil liability for distribution of sexually explicit deepfake images.

stateLaws.citation: ELVIS Act, Tenn. Code Ann. 47-25-1701 et seq.
stateLaws.scope: Extends Tennessee's right of publicity to cover AI-generated replicas of voice and likeness. Protects against unauthorized use of a person's voice or visual likeness through AI or other technology.
stateLaws.penalty: Civil liability: actual damages or profits, injunctive relief, attorney fees. Statutory damages available.
stateLaws.privateRightOfAction: Yes.

factChecks.label: Legislation
factChecks.value: The ELVIS Act (Ensuring Likeness, Voice, and Image Security Act of 2024) was enacted as HB 2091, Public Chapter 588, signed March 26, 2024 and effective July 1, 2024. The tracker previously miscited SB 769, an unrelated alcoholic-beverages bill.

stateLaws.scope: Protects deceased performers' digital replicas. Extends the right of publicity to cover unauthorized use of a deceased personality's digital replica, including AI-generated likenesses.
stateLaws.penalty: Civil liability: damages, profits, injunctive relief.
stateLaws.privateRightOfAction: Yes, representatives of deceased personalities.

factChecks.label: Bill text
factChecks.value: AB 1836 extends California's right of publicity to prohibit unauthorized use of a deceased personality's digital replica. Effective 2025-01-01.

stateLaws.scope: Requires informed consent before using AI to create digital replicas of performers in contracts. Makes contract provisions for AI replicas unenforceable if the performer was not represented or did not have a meaningful opportunity to consult representation.
stateLaws.penalty: Contract remedy: unenforceable provisions. Civil liability for unauthorized use.
stateLaws.privateRightOfAction: Yes.

factChecks.label: Bill text
factChecks.value: AB 2602 requires informed consent before using AI replicas of performers in contracts. Effective 2025-01-01.

stateLaws.citation: N.Y. Civil Rights Law 50-f (S.5959-D/A.5605-C, L. 2020 ch. 304)
stateLaws.scope: Regulates unauthorized commercial and certain expressive uses of a deceased performer's or deceased personality's highly realistic digital replica.
stateLaws.penalty: Civil damages and related remedies.
stateLaws.privateRightOfAction: Yes.

factChecks.label: Codified statute
factChecks.value: Civil Rights Law 50-f, enacted through S.5959-D/A.5605-C (L. 2020 ch. 304), governs digital replicas of deceased performers and personalities; the codified page reflects a December 19, 2025 revision.

openItems: Effective clauses of the latest amendatory bills pending confirmation against their chapter laws.

stateLaws.scope: Renders insufficiently specific contractual grants for replacing a living individual's performance with a digital replica void and unenforceable.
stateLaws.penalty: Contractual unenforceability rather than a criminal penalty.
stateLaws.privateRightOfAction: Yes, through ordinary contract litigation or defense.

factChecks.label: Codified statute
factChecks.value: General Obligations Law 5-302 voids certain contract provisions for digital replicas of living performers; the codified page reflects a January 3, 2025 revision.

changeLog.label: Wrong-bill citations corrected before state-page launch
changeLog.change: Corrected six records against official legislative sources. Louisiana now cites 2026 HB 459 (Act 806, effective August 1, 2026) instead of the unrelated 2025 HB 459, a renewable-energy law. Maryland now cites 2026 SB 141 (Chapter 444, effective June 1, 2026) instead of the withdrawn 2025 SB 141, a criminal-sentencing bill. Tennessee's ELVIS Act now cites HB 2091 (Public Chapter 588) instead of SB 769, an unrelated alcohol bill. Maine's LD 517 gained its chapter law (P.L. 2026 ch. 593) and corrected July 29, 2026 effective date. Pennsylvania's SB 649 (Act 35) was reclassified as a general digital-forgery offense rather than an election statute. Kentucky's SB 4 gained act, code section (KRS 117.322), effective date, and penalty detail. Minnesota, Florida, and Montana penalty fields were also tightened to statute-level detail.

changeLog.label: Primary-source verification pass
changeLog.change: Densified seven state election-deepfake records to full detail (Arizona, Colorado, Indiana, New Hampshire, New York, Oregon, Utah) with codified citations, effective dates, penalties, and private-right-of-action fields from primary sources.

changeLog.label: South Carolina and Virginia rows removed
changeLog.change: Removed the South Carolina and Virginia election-deepfake rows: no enacted statute could be verified in either state. South Carolina's H4660 did not become law and successor H3517 shows introduction only; the Virginia provision previously cited is a conventional sponsor-disclosure rule, not a deepfake law.

changeLog.label: New Jersey recited; New York replica record split
changeLog.change: New Jersey's record now cites the enacted general deceptive-media statute (A3540, P.L. 2025, c. 40) rather than a dedicated election bill, and stays listed under the election category because the general statute reaches deceptive election media. The New York digital-replica placeholder was split into Civil Rights Law 50-f (deceased performers) and General Obligations Law 5-302 (living-performer contracts), both with codified sources.

changeLog.label: Watch items
changeLog.change: Montana SB 25 (2025) election-deepfake bill and Vermont S.23 (2025-2026) synthetic-media bill identified as candidate additions; enactment status could not be verified at this check, so neither was added. Kohls v. Bonta and X Corp v. Bonta docket status also unverified at this pass; the existing litigation caveats stand.

changeLog.label: Initial publication
changeLog.change: Published deepfake and AI impersonation law tracker with 8 federal instruments, 30 state election-deepfake entries (12 full detail, 18 index), 4 synthetic NCII exemplars, and 4 digital replica laws.

changeLog.label: TAKE IT DOWN Act platform deadline
changeLog.change: Platform compliance deadline for the TAKE IT DOWN Act passed. FTC reporting portal (TakeItDown.ftc.gov) is operational.

changeLog.label: DEFIANCE Act (pending bill) passes Senate
changeLog.change: S.1837 passed the Senate on 2026-01-13. The bill has not been enacted into law and awaits House action.

monitoringSources.label: FTC Take It Down reporting portal
Public Citizen, Tracker: Legislation on Deepfakes in Elections (secondary source)
monitoringSources.note: Third-party tracker; state counts from this source are attributed, not independently verified.
